local_names.romanized_local: Yāo Guǒ (Mandarin); Muntiri (Tamil romanized); Kaju (Hindi romanized); Hat dieu (Vietnamese romanized)

region_of_origin: Native to northeastern Brazil (Anacardium occidentale); major processing in India and Vietnam; widely consumed across Southeast Asia including Singapore
food_subgroup: Tree nut, raw, unsalted. 1 standard serving = 30g = approximately 18 whole cashews.

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for raw cashew nut as a food item; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of raw cashews is 30g, equivalent to approximately 18 whole cashew nuts, providing approximately 166 kcal, 9.1g carbohydrate, 5.5g protein, and 13.2g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Cashews, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDCID 170162)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Direct match to USDA SR Legacy entry for raw cashew nuts (FDCID 170162). All per_100g nutrient values sourced from this entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Cashew Nuts — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~22 (low) sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for cashew nuts. Per 30g serving: approximately 9.1g available carbohydrate; GL = (22 × 9.1) / 100 ≈ 2.0 (low).

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium, high potassium and magnesium
hypertension.guidance: Raw unsalted cashews are suitable for hypertensive patients: sodium is very low at 12mg/100g, while potassium (660mg/100g) and magnesium (292mg/100g — the highest of common tree nuts) both support vasodilation and blood pressure reduction; a 30g serving contributes 198mg potassium and 87.6mg magnesium toward daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (22), low GL per serving (2.0), but moderate carbohydrate content requires portion awareness
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cashews have a low GI of ~22 and a GL of ~2 per 30g serving, making them suitable for T2DM patients at standard portions; however, carbohydrate content (~9g per 30g) is higher than almonds or walnuts (~3–4g per 30g), so patients should limit intake to one 30g serving and avoid the 60–90g social-eating portions common at CNY or Indian restaurant settings, which can deliver 18–27g carbohydrate equivalent to half a bowl of rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: MUFA-dominant fat profile, zero cholesterol, high magnesium and potassium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cashews have a favourable cardiovascular fat profile with monounsaturated fat (~23.8g/100g) dominant over saturated fat (7.8g/100g), zero cholesterol, and zero trans fat; combined with high potassium (660mg/100g) and magnesium (292mg/100g), a 30g daily serving is consistent with heart-healthy dietary patterns and may support endothelial function and blood pressure regulation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Zinc, copper, and magnesium support anti-inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cashews provide zinc (5.8mg/100g; ~15% RDI per 30g serving) which inhibits NF-κB signalling relevant to RA, copper as a cofactor for Cu/Zn superoxide dismutase and lysyl oxidase supporting cartilage integrity, and magnesium supporting anti-inflammatory enzyme pathways; a 30g daily serving is appropriate as part of an anti-inflammatory diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cashews (腰果, Yāo Guǒ; Malay: Kacang Gajus; Tamil: முந்திரி Muntiri; Hindi: काजू Kaju; Vietnamese: Hạt điều) are kidney-shaped tree nuts from Anacardium occidentale, native to northeastern Brazil and now processed primarily in India and Vietnam. In Singapore, cashews are a staple of Indian cooking (cashew korma, biryani garnish, cashew barfi), Chinese New Year snack mixes, and everyday healthy snacking. Raw cashews have a cream-coloured appearance, firm crunchy texture, and a distinctively sweet, buttery flavour. Nutritionally, cashews occupy an intermediate position among tree nuts: they are higher in carbohydrate (~9g per 30g serving) than almonds (~3g) or walnuts (~4g), making them the most glycemically relevant of the major tree nuts — though their GI of ~22 (low) produces only a small glycemic response at standard portions (GL ~2 per 30g). Their standout mineral profile includes exceptional magnesium (292mg/100g — the highest of common nuts), high potassium (660mg/100g), significant zinc (5.8mg/100g), and selenium (19.9mcg/100g). The fat profile is MUFA-dominant (~23.8g/100g monounsaturated) with moderate saturated fat (7.8g/100g) and zero cholesterol. The critical clinical counselling point is portion size: cashews are extremely easy to overeat due to their smooth texture and lack of fibre resistance, and social-eating scenarios (CNY snack bowls, Indian restaurant pre-dinner cashews) can easily reach 60–90g, delivering 18–27g carbohydrate — equivalent to half a bowl of rice — which is clinically significant for T2DM patients. One standard serving is 30g (approximately 18 whole cashews), providing 166 kcal, 5.5g protein, 13.2g fat, and 9.1g carbohydrate.

confidence_reason: All per_100g nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDCID 170162 (exact match for raw cashew nuts). GI value sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database. Ontology codes (FoodOn FOODON_03301416 and FoodEx2 A00GS) are verified direct matches. No estimation or analogy required.
notes: Monounsaturated fat (23.8g/100g) and polyunsaturated fat (7.8g/100g) values are from USDA SR Legacy FDCID 170162 fatty acid profile. Water content (5.2g/100g) from same source. Cholesterol is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — cashews are a plant food and contain no dietary cholesterol. Vitamin A, vitamin D, and vitamin B12 are confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for raw cashew nuts; these are naturally absent in this plant food. Trans fat is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. Vitamin A reported as 0.0 mcg RAE — naturally zero for this nut. Vitamin D reported as 0.0 mcg — naturally zero for this nut. Vitamin B12 reported as 0.0 mcg — naturally zero as cashews are a plant food (B12 is found only in animal products). No -1 sentinel values remain in core fields; all nutrients are fully reported from the approved source. GI source: University of Sydney GI Database, GI ~22 for cashew nuts (low). GL per 30g serving = (22 × 9.1) / 100 ≈ 2.0. Allergen note: cashews are a major tree nut allergen and must be declared on food labels in Singapore, Australia, and most jurisdictions. SNOMED CT code not identified for raw cashew nut as a food item; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient for ontological coverage.
